author: @ehcnjq
text:
Well, I run the creality CR10-S which has 50 x 50 x 50 [cm] (20x20x20 inch?) working space ...
It took me a few changes to make that printer stable in mechanics, because there were a lot of wobbely plastic-parts. I replaced all of them with milled aluminum-parts. I also applied 2 stabilation rods from the top to front-bottom site.
Now i'm really happy with it.

author: @michaelward402
text:
Another nice informative video. I am just starting out  with 3D printing so any video I find helpful is so important to the learning curve.
I actually bought a Spark3D SP1 which is very similar to the Elegoo you are showing. It took a while between other design tasks, but I managed a very acceptable print, which would not have been possible without seeing this first. The biggest difference is in the software, where it has been a learning curve in itself.

replies.author: @djavulkai
replies.text: FYI, I use Cura to slice my 3d prints, and it works great on my Mac and the PC.  Keep in mind, you will SLICE the STL file with Cura, and then copy that to a SD disk to put in your printer.  The printer takes care of the rest.  But yes, Macs are perfectly fine for 3d printing. :)

author: @seabeepirate

author: @eelcohoogendoorn8044
text: Not sure what the default nozzle size on this machine is; but note that the plastic flow rate you can get out of it is something like cubic in the size of the opening. So taking a default 0.4mm nozzle to the readily available 0.8mm or even higher, can massively speed up large low-detail prints such as these.

author: @kevinmc1111
text: Some tips: You can cut down the print time by several times by using a bigger nozzle. I use a 0.8mm for almost everything except for things with minute details that need the higher resolution of the stock 0.4mm. Even 1.0mm is perfectly doable for large prints like this. Larger nozzles also increase the impact strength of the print. You can also increase the print speed in Cura or the interface while it is running, maybe even double the default speed. Do a test print of the part and increase the speed while its running until you get artifacts and then slow it back down a little. Then use that speed in Cura for the real print. You might have to adjust the flow rate up or down a bit to compensate. Also, most of the strength is in the walls, the infill is really just to support the layers printed on top of it. So for strength use more walls, to save print time and material, use less infill.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate nice tips - this was done double speed

replies.author: @michaelward402
replies.text: Thanks, I am just starting out with 3D printing and it is these types of tips which help enormously.

author: @saiello2061
text: Some words of advice for those interested in messing around with 3D printers from someone who's spent several years playing with them and designing bespoke 3D models for prototyping, from gear mechanisms to experimental impeller designs. The trick is to hit the price/functionality sweet spot. Rule of thumb is low to midrange ( < £200 ) from a well known brand. You don't even need a well known brand, even the cheapest of 3D printers these days have reached a base level of quality where there's not much difference to choose from. Don't get lured into buying all singing, all dancing machines; fully enclosed, auto bed levelling, multi extruder, filament runout detectors, semi-automatic restart on failed prints, touch screen interface, silent running, etc, thinking that it's going to make your life easier. It might initially for those models grabbed of t'interwebs, but when things go wrong they can cause more operational frustration than benefit, to say nothing of repairing them. Your bog standard dual z-axis basic single extruder with a reasonable sized bed is good enough. There's really no substitute for just jumping in with an el cheapo and learning as you go along, it'll stand you in far better stead later on and is the only way to become expert. Like basket weaving, 3D printing is a craft and an art and needs to be treated as such to get the best out of it. Stop procrastinating and just do it, you won't look back...!👍😁.

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: Mostly agree, but I will say for a newbie it's worth getting a printer with direct drive extrusion, unfortunately most of the bottom budget printers are stuck with flawed PTFE lining design that can cause lots of issues and is potentially toxic. Glad to see Elegoo went with the DD setup.

replies.author: @saiello2061
replies.text: @DKFX1 Yes, people argue about the pros and cons of Direct Drive vs Bowden type. DD is better for the novice because its simpler. Less to go wrong and easier to deal with when it does. In my experience, the most critical part is the print not sticking to the bed and getting that first solid layer down. +90% of my problems and frustration came from the print not sticking to the glass bed during that first layer build and me believing it was due to it not being one of those fancy flexible "magnetic" types and/or levelling accuracy. Nope. Once I discovered a weak solution of PVA glue washed and dried over the print area, it solved this problem brilliantly. With the print finished and cooled, a light tap or a thin blade applied between the print and bed, would see it pop right off. It made levelling the bed a far less critical affair and turned a printer I could have happily thrown into the bin into a joy to use... 😁. Once that first layer is down, most printers will run happily on without worries... 👍.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nie advice - cheers mate

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: 3D printers are super handy for making prototype parts! Just some things: 3D printers like the one in the video cannot print "overhangs" very easily, and require either some sort of structural support either from the printing filament or from a second spool of filament specifically designed to do that. There are some really interesting 3D printers out there that can print by rotating the part or by printing at an angle which really improves the ability of the 3d printer to print overhangs and similar. Basically, when you print a part, it is important to take into consideration the best printing angle for the given part's geometry or it simply won't print properly.

author: @grendel1960a
text:
I have found 3d printers to be awesome, from printing from printing parts in a small size for a 1/12 scale model boat, to printing brackets to hold drainpipes, clips to secure the fabric on a gazebo, even gears can all be printed. I am lucky as the tricky part (the design in CAD) is easy for me. I bought my first printer many years back now (2018), and it wasnt cheap as I opted for a ready built plug and play machine, (a Davinci Ai0) but you were tied to their filament, as a machine it was awesome, did everything I wanted, more recently I got the FLsun q5 machine (really cool a round bed and 3 legs that support the print head and move it around by adjusting the height of each leg to raise lower and  move the central printhead, and that too has been awesome. anyone out there dont be afraid of them, if you can think it up and draw it up, you can print it. for example I was watching video 1725 about the aeromine, and it mentioned the naca4412 aerofoil section, so I went online, found an outline for the section of the aerofoil, traced it into fusion 36, then extruded the section to 100mm long and printed it, easy peasy it took me 10 minutes to do the CAD work, and 4 hours to print the part, it was that simple. the flsun version of cura I have already has the base settings for many types of filament (I have even printed small tyres using flexible filament).
If you are in to tinkering and sometime want that one off part, a 3d filament printer is the tool for the job.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I still have to learn CAD and I was thinking of printing the aeromine - any chance you could send me an STL file of the wing? my email is robertmurraysmith64@gmail.com if it's possible - cheers

author: @TheFlyinCanadian
text: Robert, really nice stuff !  Forgive me if I can't seem to recall if you ever made graphene "enhanced" filament but it would be interesting to see the difference in strength. Since there are miriad plastic recycling-into-filament machines out there, you could make your own filament with all sorts of interesting caracteristics. Cheers!

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: There are carbon based filament fillers... they tend to be very brittle. Also, they are very harsh on the printing nozzle, and tend to erode the nozzle very very quickly.

replies.author: @TheFlyinCanadian
replies.text: @marcfruchtman9473 Indeed, I had heard that. I remember Robert making 5% graphene plastic at some point, since graphene is much finer than carbon (black?/graphite powder?), I wonder if it will be as abrasive and how it will behave once cooled.

replies.author: @johnmcfadden9336
replies.text: @TheFlyinCanadian as graphite is used as a lubricant one would imagine it wouldn’t be a problem in the same way as carbon clumps but I seem to remember rob saying getting a good dispersion into plastics is quite difficult the plastic he made from casein with graphene is a different story  now if a similar plastic could be made that could be made that could be melted and set on demand would be AWESOME as rob would say

replies.author: @TheFlyinCanadian
replies.text: @johnmcfadden9336 Did Robert mention his casein plastic was meltable ? I think he did but my old memory may be mistaken. If so, it could be a very interesting green material to 3d print with. 😀

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @johnmcfadden9336 yea, good point about different forms. Yes, graphite would be a lubricant, but carbon fiber is definitely very abrasive, and it seems graphene is somewhere between. It's hard to say exactly where graphene would be on the abrasive list.. from my reading graphene is more abrasive than PLA, and probably would require a hardened nozzle to prevent the nozzle from being degraded quickly.

author: @themeek351
text: Yes, your in my wheelhouse now!  I have been printing for a decade now and absolutely love it!  I prefer Simplify3D, but cura has some cool new infill patterns!  Seems like your printing a little slower then I would, but better to be safe than sorry!

3D prints are great for prototypes, but probably won't last.

author: @richardspicer8253
text: Your need to get some 0.8 or 1mm nozzles now and your be churning that plastic 🤣👌

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers. mate - I may well do exactly that

replies.author: @richardspicer8253
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering will drop the print time by a massive factor

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @richardspicer8253 I can see how that would work I actually printed this at 120mm/s as the standard setting is 50-60 - so, I sacrificed a little quality but on something this big and for the intended use I figured it didn't matter that much and it halved the print time - even so it took ages

replies.author: @richardspicer8253
replies.text: @Robert Murray-Smith  that's a good print at that speed.  If you get bigger nozzle you have to slow printer down. But bigger nozzle so more plastic coming out. But still you will save more time. That model looks like a 12hour print with a 0.8 or 1mm nozzle
